Optimizing Differentiable Relaxations of Coreference Evaluation Metrics

Phong Le 1 Ivan Titov 1 , 2
1 ILLC, University of Amsterdam
2 ILCC, School of Informatics, University of Edinburgh p.le@uva.nl ititov@inf.ed.ac.

73cac45d-ba6d-4214-8dd5-0aca5c012706.label: text_chunk
73cac45d-ba6d-4214-8dd5-0aca5c012706.text:
uk

Abstract

Coreference evaluation metrics are hard to optimize directly as they are non- differentiable functions, not easily decom- posable into elementary decisions. Con- sequently, most approaches optimize ob- jectives only indirectly related to the end goal, resulting in suboptimal performance.

b0951bc8-cc88-49b0-b494-2a1284453a3c.label: text_chunk
b0951bc8-cc88-49b0-b494-2a1284453a3c.text:
Instead, we propose a differentiable re- laxation that lends itself to gradient-based optimisation, thus bypassing the need for reinforcement learning or heuristic mod- ification of cross-entropy.

 We show that by modifying the training objective of a competitive neural coreference system, we obtain a substantial gain in performance. This suggests that our approach can be re- garded as a viable alternative to using rein- forcement learning or more computation- ally expensive imitation learning.

1 Introduction

Coreference resolution is the task of identifying all mentions which refer to the same entity in a docu- ment. It has been shown beneficial in many natural language processing (NLP) applications, includ- ing question answering ( Hermann et al. , 2015 ) and information extraction ( Kehler , 1997 ), and often regarded as a prerequisite to any text understand- ing task.

bd30905d-7864-4fd3-8f23-f6c7ef0ced4e.label: text_chunk
bd30905d-7864-4fd3-8f23-f6c7ef0ced4e.text: Coreference resolution can be regarded as a clustering problem: each cluster corresponds to a single entity and consists of all its mentions in a given text. Consequently, it is natural to eval- uate predicted clusters by comparing them with the ones annotated by human experts, and this is exactly what the standard metrics (e.g., MUC, B 3 , CEAF) do. In contrast, most state-of-the- art systems are optimized to make individual co-

af8ea678-5c16-488b-9f6f-bd4d1a693214.label: text_chunk
af8ea678-5c16-488b-9f6f-bd4d1a693214.text: reference decisions, and such losses are only indi- rectly related to the metrics.One way to deal with this challenge is to op- timize directly the non-differentiable metrics us- ing reinforcement learning (RL), for example, re- lying on the REINFORCE policy gradient algo- rithm ( Williams , 1992 ).

df3ac88e-5d00-496d-98e8-07c582912e70.label: text_chunk
df3ac88e-5d00-496d-98e8-07c582912e70.text: However, this approach has not been very successful, which, as suggested by Clark and Manning ( 2016a ), is possibly due to the discrepancy between sampling decisions at training time and choosing the highest rank- ing ones at test time. A more successful alter- native is using a ‘roll-out’ stage to associate cost with possible decisions, as in Clark and Manning ( 2016a ), but it is computationally expensive.

e370277b-7621-4431-bbf1-d9d132184556.label: text_chunk
e370277b-7621-4431-bbf1-d9d132184556.text:
Imi- tation learning ( Ma et al.

 , 2014b ; Clark and Man- ning , 2015 ), though also exploiting metrics, re- quires access to an expert policy, with exact poli- cies not directly computable for the metrics of in- terest.In this work, we aim at combining the best of both worlds by proposing a simple method that can turn popular coreference evaluation metrics into differentiable functions of model parameters.

653f3b5a-1d44-4ab1-89cb-6e8298d9f188.label: text_chunk
653f3b5a-1d44-4ab1-89cb-6e8298d9f188.text: As we show, this function can be computed re- cursively using scores of individual local deci- sions, resulting in a simple and efficient estima- tion procedure. The key idea is to replace non- differentiable indicator functions (e.g. the mem- ber function I ( m ∈ S ) ) with the corresponding posterior probabilities ( p ( m ∈ S ) ) computed by the model.

22733ccd-5c1f-44e6-b78d-13bdd8e353d0.label: text_chunk
22733ccd-5c1f-44e6-b78d-13bdd8e353d0.text:
Consequently, non-differentiable func- tions used within the metrics (e.

g. the set size function | S | = P m I ( m ∈ S ) ) become differ- entiable ( | S | c = P m p ( m ∈ S ) ). Though we assume that the scores of the underlying statis- tical model can be used to define a probability model, we show that this is not a serious limita- tion.

model of Wiseman et al. ( 2015b ), which on its own outperforms the original one and achieves similar performance to its global version ( Wiseman et al. , 2016 ). Importantly when we use the introduced differentiable relaxations in training, we observe a substantial gain in performance over our prob- abilistic baseline.

 Our method to turn a mention ranking resolver into an entity-centric re- solver is presented in Section 3 , and the proposed differentiable relaxations in Section 4 . Section 5 shows our experimental results.

2.2 Evaluation Metrics

We use five most popular metrics 4 ,

• MUC ( Vilain et al. , 1995 ),

• B 3 ( Bagga and Baldwin , 1998 ),

• CEAF m , CEAF e ( Luo , 2005 ),

• BLANC ( Luo et al. , 2014 ),

• LEA ( Moosavi and Strube , 2016 ).

3 From mention ranking to entity centricity

Mention-ranking resolvers do not explicitly pro- vide information about entities/clusters which is required by B 3 and LEA.

L β,B 3 (Θ; T ) = − ˆ F β,B 3 (Θ; T ) + λ || Θ || 1 L β,LEA (Θ; T ) = − ˆ F β,LEA (Θ; T ) + λ || Θ || 1

5.3 Discussion

Because the resolvers are evaluated on F 1 score metrics, it should be that L β,B 3 and L β,LEA per- form the best with β = 1 .

Figure 4 and Table 1 however do not confirm that: β should be set with values a little bit larger than 1. There are two hy- potheses.

 First, the statistical difference between the training set and the development set leads to the case that the optimal β on one set can be sub- optimal on the other set.

6 Related work

Mention ranking and entity centricity are two main streams in the coreference resolution liter- ature.

179393ea-e013-4f55-bd80-6f9e20194e89.label: text_chunk
179393ea-e013-4f55-bd80-6f9e20194e89.text:
Mention ranking ( Denis and Baldridge , 2007 ; Durrett and Klein , 2013 ; Martschat and Strube , 2015 ; Wiseman et al. , 2015a ) considers lo- cal and independent decisions when choosing a correct antecedent for a mention.

 This approach is computationally efficient and currently domi- nant with state-of-the-art performance ( Wiseman et al.

e99c297e-a0c0-4a24-a891-620909cd57ef.label: text_chunk
e99c297e-a0c0-4a24-a891-620909cd57ef.text:
, 2016 ; Clark and Manning , 2016a ). Wise- man et al. ( 2015b ) propose to use simple neural


Figure 4: Recall, precision, F 1 (average of MUC, B 3 , CEAF e ), on the development set when training with L β,B 3 (left) and L β,LEA (right).

4d87f26a-3dbf-4a4c-881f-d41d934b7468.label: text_chunk
4d87f26a-3dbf-4a4c-881f-d41d934b7468.text:
Higher values of β yield lower precisions but higher recalls.networks to compute mention ranking scores and to use a heuristic loss to train the model. Wiseman et al.

 ( 2016 ) extend this by employing LSTMs to compute mention-chain representations which are then used to compute ranking scores. They call these representations global features . Clark and Manning ( 2016a ) build a similar resolver as in Wiseman et al.

20b29b04-5de0-4de8-8f06-e12c718082d4.label: text_chunk
20b29b04-5de0-4de8-8f06-e12c718082d4.text:
( 2015b ) but much stronger thanks to deeper neural networks and “better mention detection, more effective, hyperparameters, and more epochs of training”.

 Furthermore, using re- ward rescaling they achieve the best performance in the literature on the English and Chinese por- tions of the CoNLL 2012 dataset. Our work is built upon mention ranking by turning a mention- ranking model into an entity-centric one.

bd3ede21-aa66-4508-b4a5-6ccc84f2e2d0.label: text_chunk
bd3ede21-aa66-4508-b4a5-6ccc84f2e2d0.text:
It is worth noting that although we use the model pro- posed by Wiseman et al. ( 2015b ), any mention- ranking models can be employed.

Entity centricity ( Wellner and McCallum , 2003 ; Poon and Domingos , 2008 ; Haghighi and Klein , 2010 ; Ma et al.

7dee02c9-9129-45fd-895c-a2c410eb99c3.label: text_chunk
7dee02c9-9129-45fd-895c-a2c410eb99c3.text:
, 2014a ; Clark and Manning , 2016b ), on the other hand, incorporates entity- level information to solve the problem. The ap- proach can be top-down as in Haghighi and Klein ( 2010 ) where they propose a generative model.

 It can also be bottom-up by merging smaller clusters into bigger ones as in Clark and Manning ( 2016b ). The method proposed by Ma et al.

0942fcf5-b5dc-4d5b-877b-af47eda1a504.label: text_chunk
0942fcf5-b5dc-4d5b-877b-af47eda1a504.text:
( 2014a ) greed- ily and incrementally adds mentions to previously built clusters using a prune-and-score technique. Importantly, employing imitation learning these two methods can optimize the resolvers directly on evaluation metrics. Our work is similar to Ma et al.

 ( 2014a ) in the sense that our resolvers incre- mentally add mentions to previously built clusters.However, different from both Ma et al.

ebb58a71-1867-4b66-90ea-e41ad55865cb.label: text_chunk
ebb58a71-1867-4b66-90ea-e41ad55865cb.text: ( 2014a ); Clark and Manning ( 2016b ), our resolvers do not use any discrete decisions (e.g., merge operations). Instead, they seamlessly compute the probability that a mention refers to an entity from mention- ranking probabilities, and are optimized on differ- entiable relaxations of evaluation metrics.

5f4fb289-cf3e-4a39-838f-b6040b253ed6.label: text_chunk
5f4fb289-cf3e-4a39-838f-b6040b253ed6.text: Using differentiable relaxations of evaluation metrics as in our work is related to a line of research in reinforcement learning where a non- differentiable action-value function is replaced by a differentiable critic ( Sutton et al. , 1999 ; Silver et al. , 2014 ). The critic is trained so that it is as close to the true action-value function as possible. This technique is applied to machine translation ( Gu et al.

7b90713b-cad1-4247-847f-ad32575f0d7f.label: text_chunk
7b90713b-cad1-4247-847f-ad32575f0d7f.text:
, 2017 ) where evaluation metrics (e.

g., BLUE) are non-differentiable. A disadvantage of using critics is that there is no guarantee that the critic converges to the true evaluation metric given finite training data. In contrast, our differentiable relaxations do not need to train, and the conver- gence is guaranteed as T → 0 .

cfa8f511-cac5-483a-8c69-146033fece1a.label: text_chunk
cfa8f511-cac5-483a-8c69-146033fece1a.text:
7 Conclusions

We have proposed

• a method for turning any mention-ranking re- solver into an entity-centric one by using a recursive formula to combine scores of indi- vidual local decisions,

 and

• differentiable relaxations for two coreference evaluation metrics, B 3 and LEA.

Experimental results show that our approach out- performs the resolver by Wiseman et al.

8483e24f-bb3f-4fa2-8ae2-e01f4f8fbbf0.label: text_chunk
8483e24f-bb3f-4fa2-8ae2-e01f4f8fbbf0.text:
( 2016 ), and gains a higher improvement over the baseline
than that of Clark and Manning ( 2016a ) but with much shorter training time.

### Summary of Relationships

1. **Pointwise Mutual Information Matrix & Skipgram Model**: The Skipgram Model utilizes the Pointwise Mutual Information Matrix to analyze word relationships based on their co-occurrence in various contexts. This highlights the model's reliance on statistical measures to enhance its understanding of language.

2. **A-RANDOM & Wikipedia**: A-RANDOM employs contexts selected from Wikipedia to generate its embeddings. This indicates a significant connection between the algorithm and Wikipedia as a data source, showcasing how external knowledge bases can inform machine learning processes.

3. **Nonsymbolic Representation Learning & Tokenization-free Approaches**: Nonsymbolic representation learning leverages tokenization-free approaches to process data, moving beyond traditional tokenization methods. This relationship emphasizes a more holistic understanding of data, allowing for richer representations without the limitations imposed by conventional tokenization.

Overall, these relationships illustrate the interconnectedness of various models and approaches in natural language processing, emphasizing the importance of data sources and innovative techniques in enhancing machine learning capabilities.

The relationships outlined in the knowledge graph highlight key connections between linguistic concepts and their applications in language processing:

1. **Ngram and Form-Meaning Homomorphism**: Ngrams are sequences of words that can be analyzed through the principle of form-meaning homomorphism, indicating that these sequences not only represent linguistic forms but also convey meaning, establishing a correspondence between structure and semantics.

2. **English Text and Disambiguation**: The clarity of well-edited English text relies on disambiguation, which is essential for ensuring that the meanings of words are clear and contextually appropriate, thereby enhancing comprehension and communication.

3. **Morphological Segmentation and Word Representation Models**: Morphological segmentation plays a crucial role in word representation models by providing a framework for breaking down words into smaller units. This decomposition allows for various representations of words, facilitating better understanding and processing in linguistic applications.

Overall, these relationships emphasize the interconnectedness of linguistic structures, the importance of clarity in language, and the methodologies used to represent and analyze language effectively.
